Kali Linux & Vulnerability Assessment
Kali Linux VM · Nessus Essentials █
// about

Kali Linux as the attacker-perspective box for the lab, used to run a Nessus vulnerability scan against the Windows 11 client joined to the jflaum.cyber domain.

Kali LinuxNessus Essentials192.168.10.0/24
// kali linux vm

Deploying Kali Linux

Deployed Kali Linux as a VM alongside the Windows Server and Windows 11 guests, on its own dual-adapter setup so it could reach both the internet and the internal lab network.

  • Gave it two network adapters — NAT for internet access/updates, and a second adapter on the internal 192.168.10.0/24 network shared with DC01 and the Windows 11 client
  • Used it as the attacker-perspective box for the lab, running Nessus (with Nmap and Metasploit kept on hand) against the domain
Kali Linux alongside the Windows Server 2025 VM in VirtualBox Manager
lab network architecture — Kali bridges NAT and the internal domain network
HopDetail
Internetvia NAT (Adapter 1)
Kali LinuxNessus, Nmap, Metasploit
Internal NetworkAdapter 2 — 192.168.10.0/24
Windows Server192.168.10.10 (DC01)
Windows 11192.168.10.20 (domain-joined)
Kali LinuxVirtualBoxDual NIC
// vulnerability assessment — nessus

Set Up Nessus on Kali step 1

  • Installed Nessus Essentials on the Kali Linux VM
  • Accessed the web UI locally at localhost:8834 and signed in as jflaumcyber
Nessus Essentials running on Kali Linux

Run a Basic Network Scan step 2

  • Created a Basic Network Scan policy targeting the Windows 11 client at 192.168.10.20
  • Ran the scan unauthenticated over the internal network
  • Scan completed in about 10 minutes
scan in progress against 192.168.10.20
scan completed — 15 unique vulnerabilities found across 30 total findings

Review the Findings step 3

  • All 15 findings came back at Info severity — no critical, high, medium, or low issues, which tracks for an unauthenticated scan against a default Windows 11 install
  • Findings were mostly service/port detection — SMB, port scanners, and general OS fingerprinting
  • Drilled into individual findings like SMB service detection to see the plugin output and affected ports/hosts
15 vulnerabilities grouped by family — Windows, port scanners, service detection
drilling into the Microsoft Windows SMB Service Detection finding
Note: an unauthenticated scan only sees what's exposed externally — running this again with domain credentials would surface deeper config and patch-level findings.
Nessus EssentialsBasic Network ScanCVSS v3.0Unauthenticated
// next steps
  • Re-run the scan authenticated with domain credentials to surface deeper findings
  • Add a second scan target (DC01 itself) for comparison
  • Start working through remediation for whatever an authenticated scan turns up
// jordan flaum  ·  ← back to home lab
